**Astrology** is a range of [divinatory](/wiki/Divination "Divination") practices, recognized as [pseudoscientific](/wiki/Pseudoscientific "Pseudoscientific") since the 18th century,[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThagard1978229-2) that propose that information about human affairs and terrestrial events may be discerned by studying the apparent positions of [celestial objects](/wiki/Celestial_objects_in_astrology "Celestial objects in astrology").[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Thagard-1978-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) Different cultures have employed forms of astrology since at least the 2nd millennium BCE, these practices having originated in [calendrical](/wiki/Calendrical_calculation "Calendrical calculation") systems used to predict seasonal shifts and to interpret celestial cycles as signs of divine communications.[[8]](#cite_note-Koch-Westenholz-1995-8)

Most, if not all, cultures have attached importance to what they observed in the sky, and some—such as the [Hindus](/wiki/Hindu_astrology "Hindu astrology"), [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_astrology "Chinese astrology"), and the [Maya](/wiki/Maya_civilization "Maya civilization")—developed elaborate systems for predicting terrestrial events from celestial observations. [Western astrology](/wiki/Western_astrology "Western astrology"), one of the oldest astrological systems still in use, can trace its roots to 19th–17th century BCE [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"), from where it spread to Ancient Greece, Rome, the [Islamic world](/wiki/Astrology_in_medieval_Islam "Astrology in medieval Islam"), and eventually Central and Western Europe. Contemporary Western astrology is often associated with systems of [horoscopes](/wiki/Horoscope "Horoscope") that purport to explain aspects of a person's personality and predict significant events in their lives based on the positions of celestial objects; the majority of professional astrologers rely on such systems.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBennett200783-9)

Throughout its history, astrology has had its detractors, competitors and skeptics who opposed it for moral, religious, political, and empirical reasons.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHughes200487-10)[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPigliucci2024-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) Nonetheless, prior to [the Enlightenment](/wiki/The_Enlightenment "The Enlightenment"), astrology was generally considered a scholarly tradition and was common in learned circles, often in close relation with [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy"), [meteorology](/wiki/Astrometeorology "Astrometeorology"), [medicine](/wiki/Medical_astrology "Medical astrology"), and [alchemy](/wiki/Alchemy "Alchemy").[[13]](#cite_note-Kassell-2010-13) It was present in political circles and is mentioned in various works of literature, from [Dante Alighieri](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri") and [Geoffrey Chaucer](/wiki/Geoffrey_Chaucer "Geoffrey Chaucer") to [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare"), [Lope de Vega](/wiki/Lope_de_Vega "Lope de Vega"), and [Pedro Calderón de la Barca](/wiki/Pedro_Calder%C3%B3n_de_la_Barca "Pedro Calderón de la Barca"). During the Enlightenment, however, astrology lost its status as an area of legitimate scholarly pursuit.[[14]](#cite_note-Porter-2001-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Rutkin-2006-15)

Since the end of the 19th century and the wide-scale adoption of the [scientific method](/wiki/Scientific_method "Scientific method"), researchers have successfully challenged astrology on both theoretical[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiswasMallikVishveshwara1989249-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Reidel-1978-17) and experimental grounds[[18]](#cite_note-Carlson-1985-18)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZarka2011-19) and have shown it to have no scientific validity or [explanatory power](/wiki/Explanatory_power "Explanatory power").[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBennett2007-20) Astrology thus lost its academic and theoretical standing in the western world, and common belief in it largely declined, until a continuing resurgence starting in the 1960s.[[21]](#cite_note-Pingree-2025-21)

## Etymology

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Marcantonio_Raimondi_-_Two_Women_with_the_Signs_of_Libra_and_Scorpio.jpg/250px-Marcantonio_Raimondi_-_Two_Women_with_the_Signs_of_Libra_and_Scorpio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marcantonio_Raimondi_-_Two_Women_with_the_Signs_of_Libra_and_Scorpio.jpg)

[Marcantonio Raimondi](/wiki/Marcantonio_Raimondi "Marcantonio Raimondi") engraving, 15th century

The word *[astrology](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/astrology "wikt:astrology")* comes from the early [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") word *[astrologia](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/astrologia "wikt:astrologia")*,[[22]](#cite_note-22) which derives from the [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") [ἀστρολογία](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E1%BC%80%CF%83%CF%84%CF%81%CE%BF%CE%BB%CE%BF%CE%B3%CE%AF%CE%B1 "wikt:ἀστρολογία")—from [ἄστρον](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E1%BC%84%CF%83%CF%84%CF%81%CE%BF%CE%BD#Ancient_Greek "wikt:ἄστρον") *astron* ("star") and [-λογία](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-%CE%BB%CE%BF%CE%B3%CE%AF%CE%B1#Ancient_Greek "wikt:-λογία") *-logia*, ("study of"—"account of the stars"). The word entered the English language via Latin and [medieval French](/wiki/Medieval_French "Medieval French"), and its use overlapped considerably with that of *astronomy* (derived from the Latin *[astronomia](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/astronomia "wikt:astronomia")*). By the [17th century](/wiki/17th_century "17th century"), *astronomy* became established as the scientific term, with *astrology* referring to divinations and schemes for predicting human affairs.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## History

Main article: [History of astrology](/wiki/History_of_astrology "History of astrology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/P._26_%27The_Zodiac_Man%27_a_diagram_of_a_human_body_and_astrological_symbols_with_instructions_explaining_the_importance_of_astrology_from_a_medical_perspective.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:P._26_%27The_Zodiac_Man%27_a_diagram_of_a_human_body_and_astrological_symbols_with_instructions_explaining_the_importance_of_astrology_from_a_medical_perspective.jpg)

*The Zodiac Man*, a diagram of a human body and astrological symbols with instructions explaining the importance of astrology from a medical perspective. From a 15th-century Welsh manuscript

Many cultures have attached importance to astronomical events, and the [Indians](/wiki/Hindu_astrology "Hindu astrology"), [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_astrology "Chinese astrology"), and [Maya](/wiki/Maya_civilization "Maya civilization") developed elaborate systems for predicting terrestrial events from celestial observations. A form of astrology was practised in the [Old Babylonian](/wiki/Old_Babylonian_Empire "Old Babylonian Empire") period of [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"), c. 1800 BCE.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[8]](#cite_note-Koch-Westenholz-1995-8) *Vedāṅga Jyotiṣa* is one of earliest known Hindu texts on [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy") and astrology (*[Jyotisha](/wiki/Jyotisha "Jyotisha")*). The text is dated between 1400 BCE to final centuries BCE by various scholars according to astronomical and linguistic evidences. Chinese astrology was elaborated in the [Zhou dynasty](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty") (1046–256 BCE). [Hellenistic astrology](/wiki/Hellenistic_astrology "Hellenistic astrology") after 332 BCE mixed [Babylonian astrology](/wiki/Babylonian_astrology "Babylonian astrology") with Egyptian [Decanic astrology](/wiki/Decans "Decans") in [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria"), creating [horoscopic astrology](/wiki/Horoscopic_astrology "Horoscopic astrology"). [Alexander the Great's](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") conquest of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") allowed astrology to spread to [Ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") and [Rome](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome"). In Rome, astrology was associated with "[Chaldean](/wiki/Chaldea "Chaldea") wisdom". After the conquest of Alexandria in the 7th century, astrology was taken up by Islamic scholars, and Hellenistic texts were translated into Arabic and Persian. In the 12th century, Arabic texts were imported to Europe and [translated into Latin](/wiki/Latin_translations_of_the_12th_century "Latin translations of the 12th century"). Major astronomers including [Tycho Brahe](/wiki/Tycho_Brahe "Tycho Brahe"), [Johannes Kepler](/wiki/Johannes_Kepler "Johannes Kepler") and [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo "Galileo") practised as court astrologers. Astrological references appear in literature in the works of poets such as [Dante Alighieri](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri") and [Geoffrey Chaucer](/wiki/Geoffrey_Chaucer "Geoffrey Chaucer"), and of playwrights such as [Christopher Marlowe](/wiki/Christopher_Marlowe "Christopher Marlowe") and [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare").

Throughout most of its history, astrology was considered a scholarly tradition. It was accepted in political and academic contexts, and was connected with other studies, such as [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy"), [alchemy](/wiki/Alchemy "Alchemy"), [meteorology](/wiki/Meteorology "Meteorology"), and medicine.[[13]](#cite_note-Kassell-2010-13) At the end of the 17th century, new scientific concepts in astronomy and physics (such as [heliocentrism](/wiki/Heliocentrism "Heliocentrism") and [Newtonian mechanics](/wiki/Classical_mechanics "Classical mechanics")) called astrology into question. Astrology thus lost its academic and theoretical standing, and common belief in astrology has largely declined.[[21]](#cite_note-Pingree-2025-21)

### Ancient world

#### Ancient applications

Further information: [Babylonian astrology](/wiki/Babylonian_astrology "Babylonian astrology") and [Worship of heavenly bodies](/wiki/Worship_of_heavenly_bodies "Worship of heavenly bodies")

Astrology, in its broadest sense, is the search for meaning in the sky.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECampion20092,_3-25) Early evidence for humans making conscious attempts to measure, record, and predict seasonal changes by reference to astronomical cycles, appears as markings on bones and cave walls, which show that [lunar cycles](/wiki/Lunar_cycle "Lunar cycle") were being noted as early as 25,000 years ago.[[26]](#cite_note-Marshack-1991-26) This was a first step towards recording the Moon's influence upon tides and rivers, and towards organising a communal calendar.[[26]](#cite_note-Marshack-1991-26) Farmers addressed agricultural needs with increasing knowledge of the [constellations](/wiki/Constellations "Constellations") that appear in the different seasons—and used the rising of particular star-groups to herald annual floods or seasonal activities.[[27]](#cite_note-27) By the 3rd millennium BCE, civilisations had sophisticated awareness of celestial cycles, and may have oriented temples in alignment with [heliacal risings](/wiki/Heliacal_rising "Heliacal rising") of the stars.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Scattered evidence suggests that the oldest known astrological references are copies of texts made in the ancient world. The [Venus tablet of Ammisaduqa](/wiki/Venus_tablet_of_Ammisaduqa "Venus tablet of Ammisaduqa") is thought to have been compiled in [Babylon](/wiki/Babylon "Babylon") around 1700 BCE.[[29]](#cite_note-29) A scroll documenting an early use of [electional astrology](/wiki/Electional_astrology "Electional astrology") is doubtfully ascribed to the reign of the [Sumerian](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer") ruler [Gudea of Lagash](/wiki/Gudea_of_Lagash "Gudea of Lagash") (c. 2144 – 2124 BCE). This describes how the gods revealed to him in a dream the constellations that would be most favourable for the planned construction of a temple.[[30]](#cite_note-30) However, there is controversy about whether these were genuinely recorded at the time or merely ascribed to ancient rulers by posterity. The oldest undisputed evidence of the use of astrology as an integrated system of knowledge is therefore attributed to the records of the first dynasty of [Babylon](/wiki/Babylon "Babylon") (1950–1651 BCE). This astrology had some parallels with [Hellenistic](/wiki/Hellenistic "Hellenistic") Greek (western) astrology, including the [zodiac](/wiki/Zodiac "Zodiac"), a norming point near 9 degrees in Aries, the trine aspect, planetary exaltations, and the dodekatemoria (the twelve divisions of 30 degrees each).[[31]](#cite_note-Rochberg-Halton-1988-31) The Babylonians viewed celestial events as possible signs rather than as causes of physical events.[[31]](#cite_note-Rochberg-Halton-1988-31)

The system of [Chinese astrology](/wiki/Chinese_astrology "Chinese astrology") was elaborated during the [Zhou dynasty](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty") (1046–256 BCE) and flourished during the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") (2nd century BCE to 2nd century CE), during which all the familiar elements of traditional Chinese culture – the Yin-Yang philosophy, theory of the five elements, Heaven and Earth, Confucian morality – were brought together to formalise the philosophical principles of Chinese medicine and divination, astrology, and [alchemy](/wiki/Chinese_alchemy "Chinese alchemy").[[32]](#cite_note-32)

The ancient Arabs that inhabited the [Arabian Peninsula](/wiki/Arabian_Peninsula "Arabian Peninsula") [before the advent of Islam](/wiki/Pre-Islamic_Arabia "Pre-Islamic Arabia") used to profess a widespread belief in [fatalism](/wiki/Fatalism "Fatalism") (*ḳadar*) alongside a fearful consideration for the sky and the stars, which they held to be ultimately responsible for every phenomena that occurs on Earth and for the destiny of humankind.[[33]](#cite_note-al-Abbasi-2020-33) Accordingly, they shaped their entire lives in accordance with their interpretations of astral configurations and phenomena.[[33]](#cite_note-al-Abbasi-2020-33)

#### Ancient objections

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/M._Tullius_Cicero%2C_Capitoline_Museum%2C_Rome.jpg/250px-M._Tullius_Cicero%2C_Capitoline_Museum%2C_Rome.jpg)](/wiki/File:M._Tullius_Cicero,_Capitoline_Museum,_Rome.jpg)

The Roman orator [Cicero](/wiki/Cicero "Cicero") objected to astrology.

The [Hellenistic](/wiki/Hellenistic_philosophy "Hellenistic philosophy") schools of [philosophical skepticism](/wiki/Philosophical_skepticism "Philosophical skepticism") criticized astrology, alongside all other beliefs.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Criticism of astrology by [academic skeptics](/wiki/Academic_skepticism "Academic skepticism") such as [Carneades](/wiki/Carneades "Carneades"),[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHughes200487-10) [Cicero](/wiki/Cicero "Cicero"),[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFernandez-Beanato2020-35) and [Favorinus](/wiki/Favorinus "Favorinus");[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong2005184-36) [Pyrrhonists](/wiki/Pyrrhonism "Pyrrhonism") such as [Sextus Empiricus](/wiki/Sextus_Empiricus "Sextus Empiricus");[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong2005186-37) and [neoplatonists](/wiki/Neoplatonist "Neoplatonist") such as [Plotinus](/wiki/Plotinus "Plotinus"),[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong2005174-39) has been preserved.

[Carneades](/wiki/Carneades "Carneades") argued that belief in fate denies [free will](/wiki/Free_will "Free will") and [morality](/wiki/Morality "Morality"); that people born at different times can all die in the same accident or battle; and that contrary to uniform influences from the stars, tribes and cultures are all different.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

[Cicero](/wiki/Cicero "Cicero"), in *[De Divinatione](/wiki/De_Divinatione "De Divinatione")*, leveled a critique of astrology that some modern philosophers consider to be the first working definition of [pseudoscience](/wiki/Pseudoscience "Pseudoscience") and the answer to the [demarcation problem](/wiki/Demarcation_problem "Demarcation problem").[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFernandez-Beanato2020-35) The philosopher of science [Massimo Pigliucci](/wiki/Massimo_Pigliucci "Massimo Pigliucci"), building on the work of the historian of science, Damian Fernandez-Beanato, argues that Cicero outlined a "convincing distinction between astrology and astronomy that remains valid in the twenty-first century."[[41]](#cite_note-41) [Cicero](/wiki/Cicero "Cicero") stated the twins objection (that with close birth times, personal outcomes can be very different), later developed by [Augustine](/wiki/Augustine_of_Hippo "Augustine of Hippo").[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong2005173-42) He argued that since the other planets are much more distant from the Earth than the Moon, they could have only very tiny influence compared to the Moon's.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong2005173–174-43) He also argued that if astrology explains everything about a person's fate, then it wrongly ignores the visible effect of inherited ability and parenting, changes in health worked by medicine, or the effects of the weather on people.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong2005177-44) The historian Stefano Rapisarda notes that the text is formally "equally balanced between *pro* and *contra*, and no final or definite answer is given."[[45]](#cite_note-45)

[Favorinus](/wiki/Favorinus "Favorinus") argued that it was absurd to imagine that stars and planets would affect human bodies in the same way as they affect the tides, and equally absurd that small motions in the heavens cause large changes in people's fates.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong2005184-36)

[Sextus Empiricus](/wiki/Sextus_Empiricus "Sextus Empiricus") argued that it was absurd to link human attributes with myths about the signs of the zodiac, and wrote an entire book, *[Against the Astrologers](/wiki/Against_the_Astrologers "Against the Astrologers")* (Πρὸς ἀστρολόγους, *Pros astrologous*), compiling arguments against astrology. *Against the Astrologers* was the fifth section of a larger work arguing against philosophical and scientific inquiry in general, *Against the Professors* (Πρὸς μαθηματικούς, *Pros mathematikous*).[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong2005186-37)

[Plotinus](/wiki/Plotinus "Plotinus"), a [neoplatonist](/wiki/Neoplatonist "Neoplatonist"), had a lasting interest in astrology, including the question of how the world of humans could be affected by the stars, and (if so) whether astrology could predict events on Earth.[[46]](#cite_note-46) He argued that since the fixed stars are much more distant than the planets, it is laughable to imagine the planets' effect on human affairs should depend on their position with respect to the zodiac. He also argues that the interpretation of the Moon's [conjunction](/wiki/Conjunction_(astronomy) "Conjunction (astronomy)") with a planet as good when the moon is full, but bad when the moon is waning, is clearly wrong, as from the Moon's point of view, half of its surface is always in sunlight; and from the planet's point of view, waning should be better, as then the planet sees some light from the Moon, but when the Moon is full to us, it is dark, and therefore bad, on the side facing the planet in question.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong2005174-39)

### Hellenistic Egypt

Main article: [Hellenistic astrology](/wiki/Hellenistic_astrology "Hellenistic astrology")

[![Ptolemy's Tetrabiblos, the Hellenistic text that founded Western astrology](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Quadritpartitum.jpg/250px-Quadritpartitum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Quadritpartitum.jpg)

1484 copy of first page of [Ptolemy's](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") *Tetrabiblos*, translated into Latin by [Plato of Tivoli](/wiki/Plato_of_Tivoli "Plato of Tivoli")

In 525 BCE, [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") was conquered by the Persians. The 1st century BCE Egyptian [Dendera Zodiac](/wiki/Dendera_Zodiac "Dendera Zodiac") shares two signs – the Balance and the Scorpion – with Mesopotamian astrology.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

With the occupation by [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") in 332 BCE, Egypt became [Hellenistic](/wiki/Hellenistic "Hellenistic"). The city of [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria") was founded by Alexander after the conquest, becoming the place where [Babylonian astrology](/wiki/Babylonian_astrology "Babylonian astrology") was mixed with Egyptian [Decanic astrology](/wiki/Decans "Decans") to create [Horoscopic astrology](/wiki/Horoscopic_astrology "Horoscopic astrology"). This contained the Babylonian zodiac with its system of planetary [exaltations](/wiki/Exaltation_(astrology) "Exaltation (astrology)"), the triplicities of the signs and the importance of eclipses. It used the Egyptian concept of dividing the zodiac into thirty-six decans of ten degrees each, with an emphasis on the rising decan, and the Greek system of planetary Gods, sign rulership and [four elements](/wiki/Four_elements "Four elements").[[48]](#cite_note-48) 2nd century BCE texts predict positions of planets in zodiac signs at the time of the rising of certain decans, particularly Sothis.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarton199420-49) The [astrologer](/wiki/Astrologer "Astrologer") and astronomer [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy#Astrology "Ptolemy") lived in Alexandria. Ptolemy's work the *[Tetrabiblos](/wiki/Tetrabiblos "Tetrabiblos")* formed the basis of Western astrology, and, "...enjoyed almost the authority of a Bible among the astrological writers of a thousand years or more."[[50]](#cite_note-50)

### Greece and Rome

The conquest of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") by [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") exposed the Greeks to ideas from [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria"), Babylon, Persia and central Asia.[[51]](#cite_note-51) Around 280 BCE, [Berossus](/wiki/Berossus "Berossus"), a priest of [Bel](/wiki/Marduk "Marduk") from Babylon, moved to the Greek island of [Kos](/wiki/Kos "Kos"), teaching astrology and Babylonian culture.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECampion200884-52) By the 1st century BCE, there were two varieties of astrology, one using [horoscopes](/wiki/Horoscope "Horoscope") to describe the past, present and future; the other, [theurgic](/wiki/Theurgic "Theurgic"), emphasising the [soul's](/wiki/Soul "Soul") ascent to the stars.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECampion2008173–174-53) Greek influence played a crucial role in the transmission of astrological theory to [Rome](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome").[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarton199432-54)

Satirist [Lucian of Samasota](/wiki/Lucian "Lucian") wrote a satirical critique of the astrology in the Roman Empire. Modern scholars of the [Second Sophistic](/wiki/Second_Sophistic "Second Sophistic") consider this an early example of a rationalist polemic against "what the author considers a pseudoscience."[[55]](#cite_note-55)

The first definite reference to astrology in Rome comes from the orator [Cato](/wiki/Cato_the_Elder "Cato the Elder"), who in 160 BCE warned farm overseers against consulting with Chaldeans,[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarton199432–33-56) who were described as Babylonian 'star-gazers'.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECampion2008227–228-57) Among both Greeks and [Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome"), Babylonia (also known as [Chaldea](/wiki/Chaldea "Chaldea")) became so identified with astrology that 'Chaldean wisdom' became [synonymous](/wiki/Synonym "Synonym") with [divination](/wiki/Divination "Divination") using planets and stars.[[58]](#cite_note-58) The 2nd-century Roman poet and satirist [Juvenal](/wiki/Juvenal "Juvenal") complains about the pervasive influence of Chaldeans, saying, "Still more trusted are the Chaldaeans; every word uttered by the astrologer they will believe has come from [Hammon's](/wiki/Jupiter_(mythology) "Jupiter (mythology)") fountain."[[59]](#cite_note-59)

One of the first astrologers to bring [Hermetic astrology](/wiki/Hermetic_astrology "Hermetic astrology") to Rome was [Thrasyllus](/wiki/Thrasyllus_of_Mendes "Thrasyllus of Mendes"), astrologer to the [emperor](/wiki/Emperor "Emperor") [Tiberius](/wiki/Tiberius "Tiberius"),[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarton199432-54) the first emperor to have had a court astrologer,[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarton199443-60) though his predecessor [Augustus](/wiki/Augustus "Augustus") had used astrology to help legitimise his [Imperial](/wiki/Imperialism "Imperialism") rights.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarton199463-61)

### Medieval world

#### Hindu

Main article: [Hindu astrology](/wiki/Hindu_astrology "Hindu astrology")

The main texts upon which classical Indian astrology is based are early medieval compilations, notably the *[*Bṛhat Parāśara Horāśāstra*](/wiki/B%E1%B9%9Bhat_Par%C4%81%C5%9Bara_Hor%C4%81%C5%9B%C4%81stra "Bṛhat Parāśara Horāśāstra")*, and *[Sārāvalī](/wiki/S%C4%81r%C4%81val%C4%AB "Sārāvalī")* by *Kalyāṇavarma*.
The *Horāshastra* is a composite work of 71 chapters, of which the first part (chapters 1–51) dates to the 7th to early 8th centuries and the second part (chapters 52–71) to the later 8th century. The *Sārāvalī* likewise dates to around 800 CE.[[62]](#cite_note-62) English translations of these texts were published by N.N. Krishna Rau and V.B. Choudhari in 1963 and 1961, respectively.

#### Islamic

Main article: [Astrology in medieval Islam](/wiki/Astrology_in_medieval_Islam "Astrology in medieval Islam")

[![Image of a Latin astrological text](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Translation_of_Albumasar_Venice_1515_De_Magnis_Coniunctionibus.jpg/250px-Translation_of_Albumasar_Venice_1515_De_Magnis_Coniunctionibus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Translation_of_Albumasar_Venice_1515_De_Magnis_Coniunctionibus.jpg)

[Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") translation of [Abū Maʿshar's](/wiki/Albumasar "Albumasar") *De Magnis Coniunctionibus* ('Of the great [conjunctions](/wiki/Conjunction_(astronomy_and_astrology) "Conjunction (astronomy and astrology)")'), [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice"), 1515

Astrology was taken up by Islamic scholars[[63]](#cite_note-63) following the collapse of [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria") to the Arabs in the 7th century, and the founding of the [Abbasid empire](/wiki/Abbasid_Caliphate "Abbasid Caliphate") in the 8th. The second Abbasid [caliph](/wiki/Caliph "Caliph"), [Al Mansur](/wiki/Al_Mansur "Al Mansur") (754–775) founded the city of [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad") to act as a centre of learning, and included in its design a library-translation centre known as *Bayt al-Hikma* 'House of Wisdom', which continued to receive development from his heirs and was to provide a major impetus for Arabic-Persian translations of Hellenistic astrological texts. The early translators included [Mashallah](/wiki/Mashallah_ibn_Athari "Mashallah ibn Athari"), who helped to elect the time for the foundation of Baghdad,[[64]](#cite_note-64) and [Sahl ibn Bishr](/wiki/Sahl_ibn_Bishr "Sahl ibn Bishr"), (*a.k.a.* *Zael*), whose texts were directly influential upon later European astrologers such as [Guido Bonatti](/wiki/Guido_Bonatti "Guido Bonatti") in the 13th century, and [William Lilly](/wiki/William_Lilly "William Lilly") in the 17th century.[[65]](#cite_note-65) Knowledge of Arabic texts started to become imported into Europe during the [Latin translations of the 12th century](/wiki/Latin_translations_of_the_12th_century "Latin translations of the 12th century").

#### Jewish

Medieval [Jewish astrology](/wiki/Jewish_astrology "Jewish astrology") developed significantly in the Islamic World, where Jewish scholars studied, adapted, and debated astrological knowledge inherited from Greek and Arabic sources. While some, like [Maimonides](/wiki/Maimonides "Maimonides"), famously rejected astrology as unscientific and theologically problematic, others, including [Saadia Gaon](/wiki/Saadia_Gaon "Saadia Gaon"), [Sherira Gaon](/wiki/Sherira_ben_Hanina "Sherira ben Hanina"), and [Hai Gaon](/wiki/Hai_ben_Sherira "Hai ben Sherira"), addressed astrological ideas in their commentaries and responsa.[[66]](#cite_note-:3-66) [Dunash ibn Tamim](/wiki/Dunash_ibn_Tamim "Dunash ibn Tamim"), active in [Kairouan](/wiki/Kairouan "Kairouan"), incorporated astrology into biblical exegesis and authored a critical treatise on its principles.[[66]](#cite_note-:3-66) Astrological texts circulated widely among Jewish communities, as evidenced by hundreds of Hebrew and [Judeo-Arabic](/wiki/Judeo-Arabic "Judeo-Arabic") fragments preserved in the [Cairo Geniza](/wiki/Cairo_Geniza "Cairo Geniza"), including horoscopes, almanacs, and medical or meteorological prognostications.[[66]](#cite_note-:3-66)

The most influential figure was [Abraham Ibn Ezra](/wiki/Abraham_ibn_Ezra "Abraham ibn Ezra") (1089–1164), who was born in [Tudela](/wiki/Tudela "Tudela"), in [Al-Andalus](/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus"), and later traveled extensively throughout the Mediterranean and Western Europe. His astrological corpus includes treatises on horoscopy (*Sefer ha-She’elot*), electional astrology (*Sefer ha-Mivḥarim*), medical astrology (*Sefer ha-Me'orot*), and introductions to theory (*Reshit Ḥokhmah*, *Mishpeṭei ha-Mazalot*). His writings served as a bridge between Arabic and Latin astrological traditions and shaped Jewish and Christian astrology in medieval Europe.[[66]](#cite_note-:3-66)

#### Europe

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Meister_von_San_Vitale_in_Ravenna.jpg/250px-Meister_von_San_Vitale_in_Ravenna.jpg)](/wiki/File:Meister_von_San_Vitale_in_Ravenna.jpg)

[Dante Alighieri](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri") meets the Emperor [Justinian](/wiki/Justinian_I "Justinian I") in the Sphere of [Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(planet) "Mercury (planet)"), in Canto 5 of the *[Paradiso](/wiki/Paradiso_(Dante) "Paradiso (Dante)").*

See also: [Christian views on astrology](/wiki/Christian_views_on_astrology "Christian views on astrology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Isidor_von_Sevilla.jpeg/250px-Isidor_von_Sevilla.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Isidor_von_Sevilla.jpeg)

The medieval theologian [Isidore of Seville](/wiki/Isidore_of_Seville "Isidore of Seville") criticised the predictive part of astrology.

In the seventh century, [Isidore of Seville](/wiki/Isidore_of_Seville "Isidore of Seville") argued in his *[Etymologiae](/wiki/Etymologiae "Etymologiae")* that astronomy described the movements of the heavens, while astrology had two parts: one was scientific, describing the movements of the Sun, the Moon and the stars, while the other, making predictions, was theologically erroneous.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWood19705-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68)

The first astrological book published in Europe was the *Liber Planetis et Mundi Climatibus* ("Book of the Planets and Regions of the World"), which appeared between 1010 and 1027 AD, and may have been authored by [Gerbert of Aurillac](/wiki/Gerbert_of_Aurillac "Gerbert of Aurillac").[[69]](#cite_note-Campion44-69) [Ptolemy's](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") second century AD *[Tetrabiblos](/wiki/Tetrabiblos "Tetrabiblos")* was translated into Latin by [Plato of Tivoli](/wiki/Plato_Tiburtinus "Plato Tiburtinus") in 1138.[[69]](#cite_note-Campion44-69) The [Dominican](/wiki/Dominican_order "Dominican order") theologian [Thomas Aquinas](/wiki/Thomas_Aquinas "Thomas Aquinas") followed [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle") in proposing that the stars ruled the imperfect 'sublunary' body, while attempting to reconcile astrology with Christianity by stating that God ruled the soul.[[70]](#cite_note-70) The thirteenth century mathematician [Campanus of Novara](/wiki/Campanus_of_Novara "Campanus of Novara") is said to have devised a system of astrological houses that divides the [prime vertical](/wiki/Prime_vertical "Prime vertical") into 'houses' of equal 30° arcs,[[71]](#cite_note-Campion46-71) though the system was used earlier in the East.[[72]](#cite_note-72) The thirteenth century [astronomer](/wiki/Astronomer "Astronomer") [Guido Bonatti](/wiki/Guido_Bonatti "Guido Bonatti") wrote a textbook, the *Liber Astronomicus*, a copy of which King [Henry VII of England](/wiki/Henry_VII_of_England "Henry VII of England") owned at the end of the fifteenth century.[[71]](#cite_note-Campion46-71)

In *[Paradiso](/wiki/Paradiso_(Dante) "Paradiso (Dante)")*, the final part of the *[Divine Comedy](/wiki/Divine_Comedy "Divine Comedy")*, the Italian poet [Dante Alighieri](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri") referred "in countless details"[[73]](#cite_note-Durling-1997-73) to the astrological planets, though he adapted traditional astrology to suit his Christian viewpoint,[[73]](#cite_note-Durling-1997-73) for example using astrological thinking in his prophecies of the reform of [Christendom](/wiki/Christendom "Christendom").[[74]](#cite_note-74)

[John Gower](/wiki/John_Gower "John Gower") in the fourteenth century defined astrology as essentially limited to the making of predictions.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWood19705-67)[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76) The influence of the stars was in turn divided into natural astrology, with for example effects on tides and the growth of plants, and judicial astrology, with supposedly predictable effects on people.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWood19706-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78) The fourteenth-century sceptic [Nicole Oresme](/wiki/Nicole_Oresme "Nicole Oresme") however included astronomy as a part of astrology in his *Livre de divinacions*.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWood19708–11-79) Oresme argued that current approaches to prediction of events such as plagues, wars, and weather were inappropriate, but that such prediction was a valid field of inquiry. However, he attacked the use of astrology to choose the timing of actions (so-called interrogation and election) as wholly false, and rejected the determination of human action by the stars on grounds of free will.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWood19708–11-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80) The friar [Laurens Pignon](/w/index.php?title=Laurens_Pignon&action=edit&redlink=1 "Laurens Pignon (page does not exist)") (c. 1368–1449)[[81]](#cite_note-81) similarly rejected all forms of divination and determinism, including by the stars, in his 1411 *Contre les Devineurs*.[[82]](#cite_note-82) This was in opposition to the tradition carried by the Arab astronomer [Albumasar](/wiki/Albumasar "Albumasar") (787–886) whose *Introductorium in Astronomiam* and *De Magnis Coniunctionibus* argued the view that both individual actions and larger scale history are determined by the stars.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVeenstra1997184-83)

In the late 15th century, [Giovanni Pico della Mirandola](/wiki/Giovanni_Pico_della_Mirandola "Giovanni Pico della Mirandola") forcefully attacked astrology in *Disputationes contra Astrologos*, arguing that the heavens neither caused, nor heralded earthly events.[[84]](#cite_note-84) His contemporary, [Pietro Pomponazzi](/wiki/Pietro_Pomponazzi "Pietro Pomponazzi"), a "rationalistic and critical thinker", was much more sanguine about astrology and critical of Pico's attack.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

### Renaissance and Early Modern

See also: [Renaissance magic](/wiki/Renaissance_magic "Renaissance magic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Robert_Fludd%27s_An_Astrologer_Casting_a_Horoscope_1617.jpg/250px-Robert_Fludd%27s_An_Astrologer_Casting_a_Horoscope_1617.jpg)](/wiki/File:Robert_Fludd%27s_An_Astrologer_Casting_a_Horoscope_1617.jpg)

'An Astrologer Casting a Horoscope' from [Robert Fludd's](/wiki/Robert_Fludd "Robert Fludd") *Utriusque Cosmi Historia*, 1617

[Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") scholars commonly practised astrology. [Gerolamo Cardano](/wiki/Gerolamo_Cardano "Gerolamo Cardano") cast the horoscope of king [Edward VI of England](/wiki/Edward_VI_of_England "Edward VI of England"), while [John Dee](/wiki/John_Dee "John Dee") was the personal astrologer to queen [Elizabeth I of England](/wiki/Elizabeth_I_of_England "Elizabeth I of England"). [Catherine de Medici](/wiki/Catherine_de_Medici "Catherine de Medici") paid [Michael Nostradamus](/wiki/Michael_Nostradamus "Michael Nostradamus") in 1566 to verify the prediction of the death of her husband, king [Henry II of France](/wiki/Henry_II_of_France "Henry II of France"), made by her astrologer Lucus Gauricus. Major astronomers who practised as court astrologers included [Tycho Brahe](/wiki/Tycho_Brahe "Tycho Brahe") in the royal court of Denmark, [Johannes Kepler](/wiki/Johannes_Kepler "Johannes Kepler") to the [Habsburgs](/wiki/Habsburgs "Habsburgs"), [Galileo Galilei](/wiki/Galileo_Galilei "Galileo Galilei") to the [Medici](/wiki/Medici "Medici"), and [Giordano Bruno](/wiki/Giordano_Bruno "Giordano Bruno") who was burnt at the stake for heresy in Rome in 1600.[[86]](#cite_note-86) The distinction between astrology and astronomy was not entirely clear. Advances in astronomy were often motivated by the desire to improve the accuracy of astrology.[[87]](#cite_note-87) Kepler, for example, was driven by a belief in harmonies between Earthly and celestial affairs, yet he disparaged the activities of most astrologers as "evil-smelling dung".[[88]](#cite_note-88)

[Ephemerides](/wiki/Ephemeris "Ephemeris") with complex astrological calculations, and [almanacs](/wiki/Almanac "Almanac") interpreting celestial events for use in medicine and for choosing times to plant crops, were popular in Elizabethan England.[[89]](#cite_note-89) In 1597, the English [mathematician](/wiki/Mathematician "Mathematician") and [physician](/wiki/Physician "Physician") [Thomas Hood](/wiki/Thomas_Hood_(mathematician) "Thomas Hood (mathematician)") made a set of paper instruments that used revolving overlays to help students work out relationships between fixed stars or constellations, the midheaven, and the twelve [astrological houses](/wiki/House_(astrology) "House (astrology)").[[90]](#cite_note-Harkness-2007-90) Hood's instruments also illustrated, for pedagogical purposes, the supposed relationships between the signs of the zodiac, the planets, and the parts of the human body adherents believed were governed by the planets and signs.[[90]](#cite_note-Harkness-2007-90)[[91]](#cite_note-91) While Hood's presentation was innovative, his astrological information was largely standard and was taken from [Gerard Mercator's](/wiki/Gerard_Mercator "Gerard Mercator") astrological disc made in 1551, or a source used by Mercator.[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-93) Despite its popularity, Renaissance astrology had what historian Gabor Almasi calls "elite debate", exemplified by the polemical letters of Swiss physician [Thomas Erastus](/wiki/Thomas_Erastus "Thomas Erastus") who fought against astrology, calling it "vanity" and "superstition." Then around the time of the [new star of 1572](/wiki/SN_1572 "SN 1572") and the [comet of 1577](/wiki/Great_Comet_of_1577 "Great Comet of 1577") there began what Almasi calls an "extended epistemological reform" which began the process of excluding religion, astrology and [anthropocentrism](/wiki/Anthropocentrism "Anthropocentrism") from scientific debate.[[94]](#cite_note-94) By 1679, the yearly publication [La Connoissance des temps](/wiki/Connaissance_des_Temps "Connaissance des Temps") eschewed astrology as a legitimate topic.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

### Enlightenment period and onwards

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Seven_spiritualists_1906.jpg/250px-Seven_spiritualists_1906.jpg)](/wiki/File:Seven_spiritualists_1906.jpg)

[Middle-class](/wiki/Middle-class "Middle-class") Chicago women discuss spiritualism (1906).

During [the Enlightenment](/wiki/The_Enlightenment "The Enlightenment"), intellectual sympathy for astrology fell away, leaving only a popular following supported by cheap almanacs.[[14]](#cite_note-Porter-2001-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Rutkin-2006-15) One English almanac compiler, Richard Saunders, followed the spirit of the age by printing a derisive *Discourse on the Invalidity of Astrology*, while in France [Pierre Bayle's](/wiki/Pierre_Bayle "Pierre Bayle") *Dictionnaire* of 1697 stated that the subject was puerile.[[14]](#cite_note-Porter-2001-14) The [Anglo-Irish](/wiki/Anglo-Irish "Anglo-Irish") [satirist](/wiki/Satire "Satire") [Jonathan Swift](/wiki/Jonathan_Swift "Jonathan Swift") ridiculed the [Whig](/wiki/Whiggism "Whiggism") political astrologer [John Partridge](/wiki/John_Partridge_(astrologer) "John Partridge (astrologer)").[[14]](#cite_note-Porter-2001-14)

In the second half of the 17th century, the [Society of Astrologers](/w/index.php?title=Society_of_Astrologers&action=edit&redlink=1 "Society of Astrologers (page does not exist)") (1647–1684), a trade, educational, and social organization, sought to unite London's often fractious astrologers in the task of revitalizing astrology. Following the template of the popular "Feasts of Mathematicians" they endeavored to defend their art in the face of growing religious criticism. The Society hosted banquets, exchanged "instruments and manuscripts", proposed research projects, and funded the publication of sermons that depicted astrology as a legitimate biblical pursuit for Christians. They commissioned sermons that argued Astrology was divine, Hebraic, and scripturally supported by Bible passages about the [Magi](/wiki/Biblical_Magi "Biblical Magi") and the sons of [Seth](/wiki/Seth "Seth"). According to historian Michelle Pfeffer, "The society's public relations campaign ultimately failed." Modern historians have mostly neglected the Society of Astrologers in favor of the still extant [Royal Society](/wiki/Royal_Society "Royal Society") (1660), even though both organizations initially had some of the same members.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

Astrology saw a popular revival starting in the 19th century, as part of a general revival of [spiritualism](/wiki/Spiritualism_(beliefs) "Spiritualism (beliefs)") and—later, [New Age](/wiki/New_Age "New Age") philosophy,[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECampion2009239–249-97) and through the influence of mass media such as newspaper horoscopes.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECampion2009259–263-98) Early in the 20th century the psychiatrist [Carl Jung](/wiki/Carl_Jung "Carl Jung") developed some concepts concerning astrology,[[99]](#cite_note-99) which led to the development of [psychological astrology](/wiki/Psychological_astrology "Psychological astrology").[[100]](#cite_note-100)[[101]](#cite_note-101)[[102]](#cite_note-102)

## Principles and practice

Advocates have defined astrology as a symbolic language, an [art](/wiki/Art "Art") form, a [science](/wiki/Science "Science"), and a method of divination.[[103]](#cite_note-103)[[104]](#cite_note-104) Though most cultural astrology systems share common roots in ancient philosophies that influenced each other, many use methods that differ from those in the West. These include Hindu astrology (also known as "Indian astrology" and in modern times referred to as "Vedic astrology") and Chinese astrology, both of which have influenced the world's cultural history.

### Western

[Western astrology](/wiki/Western_astrology "Western astrology") is a form of [divination](/wiki/Divination "Divination") based on the construction of a [horoscope](/wiki/Horoscope "Horoscope") for an exact moment, such as a person's birth.[[105]](#cite_note-105) It uses the tropical zodiac, which is aligned to the [equinoctial points](/wiki/Equinox "Equinox").[[106]](#cite_note-106)

Western astrology is founded on the movements and relative positions of celestial bodies such as the Sun, Moon and planets, which are analysed by their movement through [signs](/wiki/Astrological_sign "Astrological sign") of the [zodiac](/wiki/Zodiac "Zodiac") (twelve spatial divisions of the [ecliptic](/wiki/Ecliptic "Ecliptic")) and by their [aspects](/wiki/Astrological_aspect "Astrological aspect") (based on geometric angles) relative to one another. They are also considered by their placement in [houses](/wiki/House_(astrology) "House (astrology)") (twelve spatial divisions of the sky).[[107]](#cite_note-107) Astrology's modern representation in western popular media is usually reduced to [Sun sign astrology](/wiki/Sun_sign_astrology "Sun sign astrology"), which considers only the zodiac sign of the Sun at an individual's date of birth, and represents only 1/12 of the total chart.[[108]](#cite_note-108)

The horoscope visually expresses the set of relationships for the time and place of the chosen event. These relationships are between the seven 'planets', signifying tendencies such as war and love; the twelve signs of the zodiac; and the twelve houses. Each planet is in a particular sign and a particular house at the chosen time, when observed from the chosen place, creating two kinds of relationship.[[109]](#cite_note-Kremer-1990-109) A third kind is the aspect of each planet to every other planet, where for example two planets 120° apart (in 'trine') are in a harmonious relationship, but two planets 90° apart ('square') are in a conflicted relationship.[[110]](#cite_note-110)[[111]](#cite_note-111) Together these relationships and their interpretations are said to form "...the language of the heavens speaking to learned men."[[109]](#cite_note-Kremer-1990-109)

Along with [tarot divination](/wiki/Tarot_divination "Tarot divination"), astrology is one of the core studies of [Western esotericism](/wiki/Western_esotericism "Western esotericism"), and as such has influenced systems of [magical](/wiki/Magic_(paranormal) "Magic (paranormal)") belief not only among Western esotericists and [Hermeticists](/wiki/Hermeticism "Hermeticism"), but also belief systems such as [Wicca](/wiki/Wicca "Wicca"), which have borrowed from or been influenced by the Western esoteric tradition. [Tanya Luhrmann](/wiki/Tanya_Luhrmann "Tanya Luhrmann") has said that "all magicians know something about astrology," and refers to a [table of correspondences](/wiki/Table_of_correspondences "Table of correspondences") in [Starhawk's](/wiki/Starhawk "Starhawk") *[The Spiral Dance](/wiki/The_Spiral_Dance "The Spiral Dance")*, organised by [planet](/wiki/Planets_in_astrology "Planets in astrology"), as an example of the astrological lore studied by magicians.[[112]](#cite_note-112)

### Hindu

Main article: [Hindu astrology](/wiki/Hindu_astrology "Hindu astrology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Brooklyn_Museum_-_Page_from_an_Astrological_Treatise.jpg/250px-Brooklyn_Museum_-_Page_from_an_Astrological_Treatise.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Museum_-_Page_from_an_Astrological_Treatise.jpg)

Page from an Indian astrological treatise, c. 1750

The earliest [Vedic](/wiki/Veda "Veda") text on astronomy is the *[Vedanga Jyotisha](/wiki/Vedanga_Jyotisha "Vedanga Jyotisha")*; Vedic thought later came to include astrology as well.[[113]](#cite_note-113)

Hindu natal astrology originated with Hellenistic astrology by the 3rd century BCE,[[114]](#cite_note-114)[[115]](#cite_note-115) though incorporating the Hindu lunar mansions.[[116]](#cite_note-116) The names of the signs (e.g. Greek 'Krios' for Aries, Hindi 'Kriya'), the planets (e.g. Greek 'Helios' for Sun, astrological Hindi 'Heli'), and astrological terms (e.g. Greek 'apoklima' and 'sunaphe' for declination and planetary conjunction, Hindi 'apoklima' and 'sunapha' respectively) in Varaha Mihira's texts are considered conclusive evidence of a Greek origin for Hindu astrology.[[117]](#cite_note-117) The Indian techniques may also have been augmented with some of the Babylonian techniques.[[118]](#cite_note-118)

### Chinese and East Asian

Further information: [Chinese zodiac](/wiki/Chinese_zodiac "Chinese zodiac")

[Chinese astrology](/wiki/Chinese_astrology "Chinese astrology") has a close relation with [Chinese philosophy](/wiki/Chinese_philosophy "Chinese philosophy") (theory of the three harmonies: heaven, earth and man) and uses concepts such as [yin and yang](/wiki/Yin_and_yang "Yin and yang"), the [Five phases](/wiki/Wuxing_(Chinese_philosophy) "Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)"), the 10 [Celestial stems](/wiki/Celestial_stem "Celestial stem"), the 12 [Earthly Branches](/wiki/Earthly_Branches "Earthly Branches"), and [shichen](/wiki/Chinese_units_of_measurement "Chinese units of measurement") (時辰 a form of timekeeping used for religious purposes). The early use of Chinese astrology was mainly confined to [political astrology](/wiki/History_of_astrology "History of astrology"), the observation of unusual phenomena, identification of [portents](/wiki/Omen "Omen") and the selection of auspicious days for events and decisions.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESunKistemaker199722,_85,_176-119)

The constellations of the Zodiac of western Asia and Europe were not used; instead the sky is divided into [Three Enclosures](/wiki/Chinese_constellations "Chinese constellations") (三垣 sān yuán), and [Twenty-Eight Mansions](/wiki/Twenty-Eight_Mansions "Twenty-Eight Mansions") (二十八宿 èrshíbā xiù) in twelve Ci ([十二次](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%88%86%E9%87%8E "zh:分野")).[[120]](#cite_note-120) The Chinese zodiac of twelve [animal signs](/wiki/Astrological_sign#Chinese_zodiac_signs "Astrological sign") is said to represent twelve different types of [personality](/wiki/Personality "Personality"). It is based on cycles of years, lunar months, and two-hour periods of the day (the shichen). The zodiac traditionally begins with the sign of the [Rat](/wiki/Rat_(zodiac) "Rat (zodiac)"), and the cycle proceeds through 11 other animal signs: the [Ox](/wiki/Ox_(zodiac) "Ox (zodiac)"), [Tiger](/wiki/Tiger_(zodiac) "Tiger (zodiac)"), [Rabbit](/wiki/Rabbit_(zodiac) "Rabbit (zodiac)"), [Dragon](/wiki/Dragon_(zodiac) "Dragon (zodiac)"), [Snake](/wiki/Snake_(zodiac) "Snake (zodiac)"), [Horse](/wiki/Horse_(zodiac) "Horse (zodiac)"), [Goat](/wiki/Goat_(zodiac) "Goat (zodiac)"), [Monkey](/wiki/Monkey_(zodiac) "Monkey (zodiac)"), [Rooster](/wiki/Rooster_(zodiac) "Rooster (zodiac)"), [Dog](/wiki/Dog_(zodiac) "Dog (zodiac)"), and [Pig](/wiki/Pig_(zodiac) "Pig (zodiac)").[[121]](#cite_note-121) Complex systems of predicting fate and destiny based on one's birthday, birth season, and birth hours, such as *ziping* and [Zi Wei Dou Shu](/wiki/Zi_wei_dou_shu "Zi wei dou shu") ([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 紫微斗数; [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 紫微斗數; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *zǐwēidǒushù*) are still used regularly in modern-day Chinese astrology. They do not rely on direct observations of the stars.[[122]](#cite_note-122)

The [Korean zodiac](/wiki/Korean_zodiac "Korean zodiac") is identical to the Chinese one. The [Vietnamese zodiac](/wiki/Vietnamese_zodiac "Vietnamese zodiac") is almost identical to the Chinese, except for second animal being the *[Water Buffalo](/wiki/Water_buffalo_(zodiac) "Water buffalo (zodiac)")* instead of the *[Ox](/wiki/Ox_(zodiac) "Ox (zodiac)")*, and the fourth animal the *[Cat](/wiki/Cat_(zodiac) "Cat (zodiac)")* instead of the *[Rabbit](/wiki/Rabbit_(zodiac) "Rabbit (zodiac)")*. The Japanese have since 1873 celebrated the beginning of the new year on 1 January as per the [Gregorian calendar](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar"). The Thai zodiac begins, not at [Chinese New Year](/wiki/Chinese_New_Year "Chinese New Year"), but either on the first day of the fifth month in the [Thai lunar calendar](/wiki/Thai_lunar_calendar "Thai lunar calendar"), or during the [Songkran](/wiki/Songkran_(Thailand) "Songkran (Thailand)") festival (now celebrated every 13–15 April), depending on the purpose of the use.[[123]](#cite_note-123)

## Theological viewpoints

See also: [Christian views on astrology](/wiki/Christian_views_on_astrology "Christian views on astrology"), [Jewish views on astrology](/wiki/Jewish_views_on_astrology "Jewish views on astrology"), and [Muslim views on astrology](/wiki/Muslim_views_on_astrology "Muslim views on astrology")

### Ancient

[Augustine](/wiki/Augustine_of_Hippo "Augustine of Hippo") (354–430) believed that the determinism of astrology conflicted with the Christian doctrines of man's free will and responsibility, and God not being the cause of evil,[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVeenstra1997184–185-124) but he also grounded his opposition philosophically, citing the failure of astrology to explain twins who behave differently although conceived at the same moment and born at approximately the same time.[[125]](#cite_note-Hess-2007-125)

### Medieval

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Avicenna.jpg/250px-Avicenna.jpg)](/wiki/File:Avicenna.jpg)

A drawing of [Avicenna](/wiki/Avicenna "Avicenna")

Some of the practices of astrology were contested on theological grounds by medieval Muslim astronomers such as [Al-Farabi](/wiki/Al-Farabi "Al-Farabi") (Alpharabius), [Ibn al-Haytham](/wiki/Ibn_al-Haytham "Ibn al-Haytham") (Alhazen) and [Avicenna](/wiki/Avicenna "Avicenna"). They said that the methods of astrologers conflicted with orthodox religious views of [Islamic scholars](/wiki/Ulema "Ulema"), by suggesting that the Will of God can be known and predicted.[[126]](#cite_note-126) For example, Avicenna's 'Refutation against astrology', *Risāla fī ibṭāl aḥkām al-nojūm*, argues against the practice of astrology while supporting the principle that planets may act as agents of divine causation. Avicenna considered that the movement of the planets influenced life on earth in a deterministic way, but argued against the possibility of determining the exact influence of the stars.[[127]](#cite_note-127) Essentially, Avicenna did not deny the core dogma of astrology, but denied our ability to understand it to the extent that precise and fatalistic predictions could be made from it.[[128]](#cite_note-128) [Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyya](/wiki/Ibn_Qayyim_al-Jawziyya "Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyya") (1292–1350), in his *Miftah Dar al-SaCadah*, also used [physical](/wiki/Physics "Physics") arguments in astronomy to question the practice of judicial astrology.[[129]](#cite_note-Livingston-1971-129) He recognised that the [stars](/wiki/Star "Star") are much larger than the [planets](/wiki/Planet "Planet"), and argued:

> And if you astrologers answer that it is precisely because of this distance and smallness that their influences are negligible, then why is it that you claim a great influence for the smallest heavenly body, Mercury? Why is it that you have given an influence to *al-Ra's* [the head] and *al-Dhanab* [the tail], which are two imaginary points [[ascending and descending nodes]](/wiki/Orbital_nodes "Orbital nodes")?[[129]](#cite_note-Livingston-1971-129)

### Modern

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Martin_Luther_by_Cranach-restoration.jpg/250px-Martin_Luther_by_Cranach-restoration.jpg)](/wiki/File:Martin_Luther_by_Cranach-restoration.jpg)

Martin Luther

[Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther") denounced astrology in his [*Table Talk*](/wiki/Table_Talk_(Luther) "Table Talk (Luther)"). He asked why twins like [Esau and Jacob](/wiki/Jacob_and_Esau "Jacob and Esau") had two different natures yet were born at the same time. Luther also compared astrologers to those who say their dice will always land on a certain number. Although the dice may roll on the number a couple of times, the predictor is silent for all the times the dice fails to land on that number.[[130]](#cite_note-Luther-2017-130)

> What is done by God, ought not to be ascribed to the stars. The upright and true Christian religion opposes and confutes all such fables.[[130]](#cite_note-Luther-2017-130)

— Martin Luther, *Table Talk*

The [Catechism of the Catholic Church](/wiki/Catechism_of_the_Catholic_Church "Catechism of the Catholic Church") maintains that divination, including predictive astrology, is incompatible with modern [Catholic](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism") beliefs[[131]](#cite_note-131) such as free will:[[125]](#cite_note-Hess-2007-125)

> All forms of divination are to be rejected: recourse to Satan or demons, conjuring up the dead or other practices falsely supposed to "unveil" the future. Consulting horoscopes, astrology, palm reading, interpretation of omens and lots, the phenomena of clairvoyance, and recourse to mediums all conceal a desire for power over time, history, and, in the last analysis, other human beings, as well as a wish to conciliate hidden powers. They contradict the honor, respect, and loving fear that we owe to God alone.[[132]](#cite_note-132)

— Catechism of the Catholic Church
