[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Karl_Popper.jpg/250px-Karl_Popper.jpg)](/wiki/File:Karl_Popper.jpg)

Popper proposed falsifiability as something that distinguishes science from non-science, using astrology as the example of an idea that has not dealt with falsification during experiment.

The scientific community rejects astrology as having no explanatory power for describing the universe, and considers it a [pseudoscience](/wiki/Pseudoscience "Pseudoscience").[[133]](#cite_note-133)[[134]](#cite_note-134)[[135]](#cite_note-135) Scientific testing of astrology has been conducted, and no evidence has been found to support any of the premises or purported effects outlined in astrological traditions.[[136]](#cite_note-136)[[137]](#cite_note-137)[[138]](#cite_note-138) There is no proposed [mechanism of action](/wiki/Scientific_modelling "Scientific modelling") by which the positions and motions of stars and planets could affect people and events on Earth that does not contradict basic and well understood aspects of biology and physics.[[139]](#cite_note-139)[[17]](#cite_note-Reidel-1978-17) Those who have faith in astrology have been characterised by scientists including Bart J. Bok as doing so "...in spite of the fact that there is no verified scientific basis for their beliefs, and indeed that there is strong evidence to the contrary".[[140]](#cite_note-140)

[Confirmation bias](/wiki/Confirmation_bias "Confirmation bias") is a form of [cognitive bias](/wiki/Cognitive_bias "Cognitive bias"), a [psychological](/wiki/Psychology "Psychology") factor that contributes to belief in astrology.[[141]](#cite_note-141)[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENickerson1998180–181-142)[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEysenckNias198242–48-143)[[144]](#cite_note-144)[[a]](#cite_note-145) Astrology believers tend to selectively remember predictions that turn out to be true, and do not remember those that turn out false. Another, separate, form of confirmation bias also plays a role, where believers often fail to distinguish between messages that demonstrate special ability and those that do not.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENickerson1998180–181-142) Thus there are two distinct forms of confirmation bias that are under study with respect to astrological belief.[[145]](#cite_note-146)

### Demarcation

Under the criterion of [falsifiability](/wiki/Falsifiability "Falsifiability"), first proposed by the [philosopher of science](/wiki/Philosopher_of_science "Philosopher of science") [Karl Popper](/wiki/Karl_Popper "Karl Popper"), astrology is a pseudoscience.[[146]](#cite_note-147) Popper regarded astrology as "pseudo-empirical" in that "it appeals to observation and experiment," but "nevertheless does not come up to scientific standards."[[147]](#cite_note-148) In contrast to scientific disciplines, astrology has not responded to falsification through experiment.[[148]](#cite_note-149): 206

In contrast to Popper, the philosopher [Thomas Kuhn](/wiki/Thomas_Kuhn "Thomas Kuhn") argued that it was not lack of falsifiability that makes astrology unscientific, but rather that the process and concepts of astrology are non-empirical.[[149]](#cite_note-Wright-1975-150): 401 Kuhn thought that, though astrologers had, historically, made predictions that categorically failed, this in itself does not make astrology unscientific, nor do attempts by astrologers to explain away failures by saying that creating a horoscope is very difficult. Rather, in Kuhn's eyes, astrology is not science because it was always more akin to [medieval medicine](/wiki/Medieval_medicine_of_Western_Europe "Medieval medicine of Western Europe"); astrologers followed a sequence of rules and guidelines for a seemingly necessary field with known shortcomings, but they did no research because the fields are not amenable to research,[[150]](#cite_note-Kuhn-1970-151): 8 and so "they had no puzzles to solve and therefore no science to practise."[[149]](#cite_note-Wright-1975-150): 401,[[150]](#cite_note-Kuhn-1970-151): 8 While an astronomer could correct for failure, an astrologer could not. An astrologer could only explain away failure but could not revise the astrological [hypothesis](/wiki/Hypothesis "Hypothesis") in a meaningful way. As such, to Kuhn, even if the stars could influence the path of humans through life, astrology is not scientific.[[150]](#cite_note-Kuhn-1970-151): 8

The philosopher [Paul Thagard](/wiki/Paul_Thagard "Paul Thagard") asserts that astrology cannot be regarded as falsified in this sense until it has been replaced with a successor. In the case of predicting behaviour, psychology is the alternative.[[6]](#cite_note-Thagard-1978-6): 228 To Thagard a further criterion of demarcation of science from pseudoscience is that the state-of-the-art must progress and that the community of researchers should be attempting to compare the current theory to alternatives, and not be "selective in considering confirmations and disconfirmations."[[6]](#cite_note-Thagard-1978-6): 227–228 Progress is defined here as explaining new phenomena and solving existing problems, yet astrology has failed to progress having only changed little in nearly 2000 years.[[6]](#cite_note-Thagard-1978-6): 228[[151]](#cite_note-Hurley-2005-152): 549 To Thagard, astrologers are acting as though engaged in [normal science](/wiki/Normal_science "Normal science") believing that the foundations of astrology were well established despite the "many unsolved problems", and in the face of better alternative theories (psychology). For these reasons Thagard views astrology as pseudoscience.[[6]](#cite_note-Thagard-1978-6)[[151]](#cite_note-Hurley-2005-152): 228

For the philosopher Edward W. James, astrology is irrational not because of the numerous problems with mechanisms and falsification due to experiments, but because an analysis of the astrological literature shows that it is infused with fallacious logic and poor reasoning.[[152]](#cite_note-James-1982-153): 34

> What if throughout astrological writings we meet little appreciation of coherence, blatant insensitivity to evidence, no sense of a hierarchy of reasons, slight command over the contextual force of critieria, stubborn unwillingness to pursue an argument where it leads, stark naivete concerning the efficacy of explanation and so on? In that case, I think, we are perfectly justified in rejecting astrology as irrational. ... Astrology simply fails to meet the multifarious demands of legitimate reasoning.

— Edward W. James[[152]](#cite_note-James-1982-153): 34

### Effectiveness

Astrology has not demonstrated its effectiveness in [controlled studies](/wiki/Experiment "Experiment") and has no scientific validity.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBennett200785-154)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZarka2011-19) Where it has made [falsifiable](/wiki/Falsifiable "Falsifiable") predictions under [controlled conditions](/wiki/Scientific_control "Scientific control"), they have been falsified.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZarka2011424-155) One famous experiment included 28 astrologers who were asked to match over a hundred natal charts to psychological profiles generated by the [California Psychological Inventory](/wiki/California_Psychological_Inventory "California Psychological Inventory") (CPI) questionnaire.[[155]](#cite_note-156)[[156]](#cite_note-Smith-2010-157) The [double-blind](/wiki/Blind_experiment#Double-blind_trials "Blind experiment") experimental protocol used in this study was agreed upon by a group of physicists and a group of astrologers[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZarka2011-19) nominated by the [National Council for Geocosmic Research](/wiki/National_Council_for_Geocosmic_Research "National Council for Geocosmic Research"), who advised the experimenters, helped ensure that the test was fair[[18]](#cite_note-Carlson-1985-18): 420,[[156]](#cite_note-Smith-2010-157): 117 and helped draw the central proposition of [natal astrology](/wiki/Natal_astrology "Natal astrology") to be tested.[[18]](#cite_note-Carlson-1985-18): 419 They also chose 26 out of the 28 astrologers for the tests (two more volunteered afterwards).[[18]](#cite_note-Carlson-1985-18): 420 The study, published in [*Nature*](/wiki/Nature_(journal) "Nature (journal)") in 1985, found that predictions based on natal astrology were no better than chance, and that the testing "...clearly refutes the astrological hypothesis."[[18]](#cite_note-Carlson-1985-18)

In 1955, the astrologer and psychologist Michel Gauquelin stated that though he had failed to find evidence that supported indicators like [zodiacal signs](/wiki/Astrological_signs "Astrological signs") and [planetary aspects](/wiki/Astrological_aspects "Astrological aspects") in astrology, he did find positive correlations between the [diurnal positions](/wiki/Diurnal_motion "Diurnal motion") of some [planets](/wiki/Planets_in_astrology "Planets in astrology") and success in professions that astrology traditionally associates with those planets.[[157]](#cite_note-158)[[158]](#cite_note-159) The best-known of Gauquelin's findings is based on the positions of Mars in the [natal charts](/wiki/Natal_chart "Natal chart") of successful athletes and became known as the *[Mars effect](/wiki/Mars_effect "Mars effect")*.[[159]](#cite_note-Carroll-2003-160): 213 A study conducted by seven French scientists attempted to replicate the claim, but found no statistical evidence.[[159]](#cite_note-Carroll-2003-160): 213–214 They attributed the effect to selective bias on Gauquelin's part, accusing him of attempting to persuade them to add or delete names from their study.[[160]](#cite_note-161)

Geoffrey Dean has suggested that the effect may be caused by self-reporting of birth dates by parents rather than any issue with the study by Gauquelin. The suggestion is that a small subset of the parents may have had changed birth times to be consistent with better astrological charts for a related profession. The number of births under astrologically undesirable conditions was also lower, indicating that parents choose dates and times to suit their beliefs. The sample group was taken from a time when belief in astrology was more common. Gauquelin had failed to find the Mars effect in more recent populations, where a nurse or doctor recorded the birth information.[[156]](#cite_note-Smith-2010-157): 116

Dean, a scientist and former astrologer, and psychologist Ivan Kelly conducted a large scale scientific test that involved more than one hundred [cognitive](/wiki/Cognitive "Cognitive"), [behavioural](/wiki/Behavioural "Behavioural"), [physical](/wiki/Physiology "Physiology"), and other variables—but found no support for astrology.[[161]](#cite_note-162)[[162]](#cite_note-Dean-2003-163) Furthermore, a [meta-analysis](/wiki/Meta-analysis "Meta-analysis") pooled 40 studies that involved 700 astrologers and over 1,000 birth charts. Ten of the tests—which involved 300 participants—had the astrologers pick the correct chart interpretation out of a number of others that were not the astrologically correct chart interpretation (usually three to five others). When date and other obvious clues were removed, no significant results suggested there was any preferred chart.[[162]](#cite_note-Dean-2003-163)

### Lack of mechanisms and consistency

Testing the validity of astrology can be difficult, because there is no consensus amongst astrologers as to what astrology is or what it can predict.[[163]](#cite_note-164) Most professional astrologers are paid to predict the future or describe a person's personality and life, but most horoscopes only make vague untestable statements that can apply to almost anyone.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBennett2007-20)[[164]](#cite_note-165)

Many astrologers believe that astrology is scientific,[[165]](#cite_note-Chris-2012-166) while some have proposed conventional [causal agents](/wiki/Causality "Causality") such as [electromagnetism](/wiki/Electromagnetism "Electromagnetism") and [gravity](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity").[[165]](#cite_note-Chris-2012-166) Scientists reject these mechanisms as implausible[[165]](#cite_note-Chris-2012-166) since, for example, the magnetic field, when measured from Earth, of a large but distant planet such as Jupiter is far smaller than that produced by ordinary household appliances.[[166]](#cite_note-167)

Western astrology has taken the earth's [axial precession (also called precession of the equinoxes)](/wiki/Precession#Axial_precession_(precession_of_the_equinoxes) "Precession") into account since Ptolemy's *[Almagest](/wiki/Almagest "Almagest")*, so the "first point of Aries", the start of the astrological year, continually moves against the background of the stars.[[167]](#cite_note-168) The tropical zodiac has no connection to the stars; tropical astrologers distinguish the constellations from their historically associated [sign](/wiki/Astrological_sign "Astrological sign"), thereby avoiding complications involving precession.[[168]](#cite_note-Charpak-2004-169) Charpak and Broch, noting this, referred to astrology based on the tropical zodiac as being "...empty boxes that have nothing to do with anything and are devoid of any consistency or correspondence with the stars."[[168]](#cite_note-Charpak-2004-169) Sole use of the tropical zodiac is inconsistent with references made, by the same astrologers, to the [Age of Aquarius](/wiki/Age_of_Aquarius "Age of Aquarius"), which depends on when the vernal point enters the constellation of Aquarius.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZarka2011-19)

Astrologers usually have only a small knowledge of astronomy, and often do not take into account basic principles—such as the precession of the equinoxes, which changes the position of the Sun with time. They commented on the example of [Élizabeth Teissier](/wiki/%C3%89lizabeth_Teissier "Élizabeth Teissier"), who wrote that "The sun ends up in the same place in the sky on the same date each year" as the basis for the idea that two people with the same birthday, but a number of years apart, should be under the same planetary influence. Charpak and Broch noted that, "There is a difference of about twenty-two thousand miles between Earth's location on any specific date in two successive years", and that thus they should not be under the same influence according to astrology. Over a 40-year period there would be a difference greater than 780,000 miles.[[168]](#cite_note-Charpak-2004-169)

### Reception in the social sciences

The general consensus of astronomers and other natural scientists is that astrology is a pseudoscience which carries no predictive capability, with many philosophers of science considering it a "paradigm or prime example of pseudoscience."[[169]](#cite_note-170) Some scholars in the social sciences have cautioned against categorizing astrology, especially ancient astrology, as "just" a pseudoscience or projecting the distinction backwards into the past.[[170]](#cite_note-171) Thagard, while demarcating it as a pseudoscience, notes that astrology "should be judged as not pseudoscientific in classical or Renaissance times...Only when the historical and social aspects of science are neglected does it become plausible that pseudoscience is an unchanging category."[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThagard1978-172) Historians of science such as Tamsyn Barton, [Roger Beck](/wiki/Roger_Beck "Roger Beck"), [Francesca Rochberg](/wiki/Francesca_Rochberg "Francesca Rochberg"), and [Wouter J. Hanegraaff](/wiki/Wouter_J._Hanegraaff "Wouter J. Hanegraaff") argue that such a wholesale description is anachronistic when applied to historical contexts, stressing that astrology was not pseudoscience before the 18th century and the importance of the discipline to the development of medieval science.[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarton1994-173)[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanegraaff2012-174)[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeck2007-175)[[175]](#cite_note-176)[[176]](#cite_note-177) R. J. Hakinson writes in the context of [Hellenistic astrology](/wiki/Hellenistic_astrology "Hellenistic astrology") that "the belief in the possibility of [astrology] was, at least some of the time, the result of careful reflection on the nature and structure of the universe."[[177]](#cite_note-178)

[Nicholas Campion](/wiki/Nicholas_Campion "Nicholas Campion"), both an astrologer and academic historian of astrology, argues that [Indigenous astronomy](/wiki/Cultural_astronomy "Cultural astronomy") is largely used as a synonym for astrology in academia, and that modern Indian and Western astrology are better understood as modes of cultural astronomy or [ethnoastronomy](/wiki/Ethnoastronomy "Ethnoastronomy").[[178]](#cite_note-179) Roy Willis and [Patrick Curry](/wiki/Patrick_Curry "Patrick Curry") draw a distinction between propositional *[episteme](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E1%BC%90%CF%80%CE%B9%CF%83%CF%84%CE%AE%CE%BC%CE%B7 "wiktionary:ἐπιστήμη")* and metaphoric *[metis](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CE%BC%E1%BF%86%CF%84%CE%B9%CF%82 "wiktionary:μῆτις")* in the ancient world, identifying astrology with the latter and noting that the central concern of astrology "is not knowledge (factual, let alone scientific) but *wisdom* (ethical, spiritual and pragmatic)".[[179]](#cite_note-180) Similarly, historian of science Justin Niermeier-Dohoney writes that astrology was "more than simply a science of prediction using the stars and comprised a vast body of beliefs, knowledge, and practices with the overarching theme of understanding the relationship between humanity and the rest of the cosmos through an interpretation of stellar, solar, lunar, and planetary movement." Scholars such as [Assyriologist](/wiki/Assyriologist "Assyriologist") Matthew Rutz have begun using the term "astral knowledge" rather than astrology "to better describe a category of beliefs and practices much broader than the term 'astrology' can capture."[[180]](#cite_note-181)[[181]](#cite_note-182)

## Cultural impact

### Western politics and society

In the West, political leaders have sometimes consulted astrologers. For example, the British intelligence agency [MI5](/wiki/MI5 "MI5") employed [Louis de Wohl](/wiki/Louis_de_Wohl "Louis de Wohl") as an astrologer after it was reported that [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") used astrology to time his actions. The War Office was "...interested to know what Hitler's own astrologers would be telling him from week to week."[[182]](#cite_note-183) In fact, de Wohl's predictions were so inaccurate that he was soon labelled a "complete charlatan", and later evidence showed that Hitler considered astrology "complete nonsense".[[183]](#cite_note-184) After [John Hinckley's](/wiki/John_Hinckley "John Hinckley") [attempted assassination](/wiki/Attempted_assassination_of_Ronald_Reagan "Attempted assassination of Ronald Reagan") of US President [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan"), first lady [Nancy Reagan](/wiki/Nancy_Reagan "Nancy Reagan") commissioned astrologer [Joan Quigley](/wiki/Joan_Quigley "Joan Quigley") to act as the secret White House astrologer. However, Quigley's role ended in 1988 when it became public through the memoirs of former chief of staff [Donald Regan](/wiki/Donald_Regan "Donald Regan").[[184]](#cite_note-185)[[185]](#cite_note-186)[[186]](#cite_note-187)

There was a boom in interest in astrology in the late 1960s. The sociologist [Marcello Truzzi](/wiki/Marcello_Truzzi "Marcello Truzzi") described three levels of involvement of "Astrology-believers" to account for its revived popularity in the face of scientific discrediting. He found that most astrology-believers did not think that it was a scientific explanation with predictive power. Instead, those superficially involved, knowing "next to nothing" about astrology's 'mechanics', read newspaper astrology columns, and could benefit from "tension-management of anxieties" and "a cognitive belief-system that transcends science."[[187]](#cite_note-Truzzi-1972-188) Those at the second level usually had their horoscopes cast and sought advice and predictions. They were much younger than those at the first level, and could benefit from knowledge of the language of astrology and the resulting ability to belong to a coherent and exclusive group. Those at the third level were highly involved and usually cast horoscopes for themselves. Astrology provided this small minority of astrology-believers with a "*meaningful* view of their universe and [gave] them an *understanding* of their place in it."[[b]](#cite_note-189) This third group took astrology seriously, possibly as an overarching religious worldview (a *sacred canopy*, in [Peter L. Berger](/wiki/Peter_L._Berger "Peter L. Berger")'s phrase), whereas the other two groups took it playfully and irreverently.[[187]](#cite_note-Truzzi-1972-188)

In 1953, the sociologist [Theodor W. Adorno](/wiki/Theodor_W._Adorno "Theodor W. Adorno") conducted a study of the astrology column of a Los Angeles newspaper as part of a project examining mass culture in capitalist society.[[188]](#cite_note-Nederman-1981-190): 326 Adorno believed that popular astrology, as a device, invariably leads to statements that encouraged conformity—and that astrologers who go against conformity, by discouraging performance at work etc., risk losing their jobs.[[188]](#cite_note-Nederman-1981-190): 327 Adorno concluded that astrology is a large-scale manifestation of systematic [irrationalism](/wiki/Irrationalism "Irrationalism"), where individuals are subtly led—through flattery and vague generalisations—to believe that the author of the column is addressing them directly.[[189]](#cite_note-191) Adorno drew a parallel with the phrase [opium of the people](/wiki/Opium_of_the_people "Opium of the people"), by Karl Marx, by commenting, "occultism is the metaphysic of the dopes."[[188]](#cite_note-Nederman-1981-190): 329

A 2005 [Gallup](/wiki/Gallup_(company) "Gallup (company)") poll and a 2009 survey by the [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") reported that 25% of US adults believe in astrology,[[190]](#cite_note-192)[[191]](#cite_note-193) while a 2024 Pew survey found a figure of 27%.[[192]](#cite_note-194) According to data released in the [National Science Foundation's](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation "National Science Foundation") 2014 *Science and Engineering Indicators* study, "Fewer Americans rejected astrology in 2012 than in recent years."[[193]](#cite_note-NSF-195) The NSF study noted that in 2012, "slightly more than half of Americans said that astrology was 'not at all scientific,' whereas nearly two-thirds gave this response in 2010. The comparable percentage has not been this low since 1983."[[193]](#cite_note-NSF-195) Astrology [apps](/wiki/Mobile_app "Mobile app") became popular in the late 2010s, some receiving millions of dollars in [Silicon Valley](/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley") [venture capital](/wiki/Venture_capital "Venture capital").[[194]](#cite_note-196)

### India and Japan

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Bdrates_of_Japan_since_1950.svg/250px-Bdrates_of_Japan_since_1950.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Bdrates_of_Japan_since_1950.svg)

Birth (in blue) and death (in red) rates of Japan since 1950, with the sudden drop in births during hinoeuma year (1966)

In India, there is a long-established and widespread belief in astrology. It is commonly used for daily life, particularly in matters concerning marriage and career, and makes extensive use of [electional](/wiki/Electional_astrology "Electional astrology"), [horary](/wiki/Horary_astrology "Horary astrology") and karmic astrology, a vertent who believes in [reincarnation](/wiki/Reincarnation "Reincarnation") and in the idea they can read a person's [karma](/wiki/Karma "Karma") in a [Natal chart](/wiki/Natal_chart "Natal chart") by studying in particular [Lunar nodes](/wiki/Lunar_node "Lunar node") and [retrograde planets](/wiki/Apparent_retrograde_motion "Apparent retrograde motion").[[195]](#cite_note-197)[[196]](#cite_note-198) Indian politics have also been influenced by astrology.[[197]](#cite_note-199) It is still considered a branch of the [Vedanga](/wiki/Vedanga "Vedanga").[[198]](#cite_note-200)[[199]](#cite_note-201) In 2001, Indian scientists and politicians debated and critiqued a proposal to use state money to fund research into astrology,[[200]](#cite_note-202) resulting in permission for [Indian universities](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_India "List of universities in India") to offer courses in Vedic astrology.[[201]](#cite_note-203)

In February 2011, the [Bombay High Court](/wiki/Bombay_High_Court "Bombay High Court") reaffirmed astrology's standing in India when it dismissed a case that challenged its status as a science.[[202]](#cite_note-204)

In [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), strong belief in astrology has led to dramatic changes in the fertility rate and the number of abortions in the years of [Fire](/wiki/Fire_(wuxing) "Fire (wuxing)") [Horse](/wiki/Horse_(zodiac) "Horse (zodiac)"). Adherents believe that women born in [*hinoeuma*](/wiki/Bingwu "Bingwu") years are unmarriageable and bring bad luck to their father or husband. In 1966, the number of babies born in Japan dropped by over 25% as parents tried to avoid the stigma of having a daughter born in the hinoeuma year.[[203]](#cite_note-205)[[204]](#cite_note-206)

### Literature and music

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Woman_in_the_Moon.jpg/250px-Woman_in_the_Moon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Woman_in_the_Moon.jpg)

Title page of [John Lyly's](/wiki/John_Lyly "John Lyly") astrological play, *The Woman in the Moon*, 1597

The fourteenth-century English poets [John Gower](/wiki/John_Gower "John Gower") and [Geoffrey Chaucer](/wiki/Geoffrey_Chaucer "Geoffrey Chaucer") both referred to astrology in their works, including Gower's *[Confessio Amantis](/wiki/Confessio_Amantis "Confessio Amantis")* and Chaucer's *[The Canterbury Tales](/wiki/The_Canterbury_Tales "The Canterbury Tales")*.[[205]](#cite_note-Wedel-2003-207) Chaucer commented explicitly on astrology in his *Treatise on the Astrolabe*, demonstrating personal knowledge of one area, judicial astrology, with an account of how to find the ascendant or rising sign.[[206]](#cite_note-208)

In the fifteenth century, references to astrology, such as with [similes](/wiki/Simile "Simile"), became "a matter of course" in English literature.[[205]](#cite_note-Wedel-2003-207)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Astrologo_Fingido_Calderon_de_la_Barca_title_page_1641.jpg/250px-Astrologo_Fingido_Calderon_de_la_Barca_title_page_1641.jpg)](/wiki/File:Astrologo_Fingido_Calderon_de_la_Barca_title_page_1641.jpg)

Title page of [Calderón de la Barca](/wiki/Pedro_Calder%C3%B3n_de_la_Barca "Pedro Calderón de la Barca")'s *Astrologo Fingido*, Madrid, 1641

In the sixteenth century, John Lyly's 1597 play, *The Woman in the Moon*, is wholly motivated by astrology,[[207]](#cite_note-De_Lacy-1934-209) while [Christopher Marlowe](/wiki/Christopher_Marlowe "Christopher Marlowe") makes astrological references in his plays *[Doctor Faustus](/wiki/Doctor_Faustus_(play) "Doctor Faustus (play)")* and *[Tamburlaine](/wiki/Tamburlaine "Tamburlaine")* (both c. 1590),[[207]](#cite_note-De_Lacy-1934-209) and [Sir Philip Sidney](/wiki/Sir_Philip_Sidney "Sir Philip Sidney") refers to astrology at least four times in his [romance](/wiki/Romance_(heroic_literature) "Romance (heroic literature)") *[The Countess of Pembroke's Arcadia](/wiki/The_Countess_of_Pembroke%27s_Arcadia "The Countess of Pembroke's Arcadia")* (c. 1580).[[207]](#cite_note-De_Lacy-1934-209) [Edmund Spenser](/wiki/Edmund_Spenser "Edmund Spenser") uses astrology both decoratively and causally in his poetry, revealing "...unmistakably an abiding interest in the art, an interest shared by a large number of his contemporaries."[[207]](#cite_note-De_Lacy-1934-209) [George Chapman's](/wiki/George_Chapman "George Chapman") play, *[Byron's Conspiracy](/wiki/The_Conspiracy_and_Tragedy_of_Charles,_Duke_of_Byron "The Conspiracy and Tragedy of Charles, Duke of Byron")* (1608), similarly uses astrology as a causal mechanism in the drama.[[208]](#cite_note-Carroll-1933-210) [William Shakespeare's](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare") attitude towards astrology is unclear, with contradictory references in plays including *[King Lear](/wiki/King_Lear "King Lear")*, *[Antony and Cleopatra](/wiki/Antony_and_Cleopatra "Antony and Cleopatra")*, and *[Richard II](/wiki/Richard_II_(play) "Richard II (play)")*.[[208]](#cite_note-Carroll-1933-210) Shakespeare was familiar with astrology and made use of his knowledge of astrology in nearly every play he wrote,[[208]](#cite_note-Carroll-1933-210) assuming a basic familiarity with the subject in his commercial audience.[[208]](#cite_note-Carroll-1933-210) Outside theatre, the physician and mystic [Robert Fludd](/wiki/Robert_Fludd "Robert Fludd") practised astrology, as did the quack doctor Simon Forman.[[208]](#cite_note-Carroll-1933-210) In Elizabethan England, "The usual feeling about astrology ... [was] that it is the most useful of the sciences."[[208]](#cite_note-Carroll-1933-210)

In seventeenth century Spain, [Lope de Vega](/wiki/Lope_de_Vega "Lope de Vega"), with a detailed knowledge of astronomy, wrote plays that ridicule astrology. In his pastoral romance *La Arcadia* (1598), it leads to absurdity; in his novela *Guzman el Bravo* (1624), he concludes that the stars were made for man, not man for the stars.[[209]](#cite_note-211) [Calderón de la Barca](/wiki/Pedro_Calder%C3%B3n_de_la_Barca "Pedro Calderón de la Barca") wrote the 1641 comedy *Astrologo Fingido* (The Pretended Astrologer); the plot was borrowed by the French playwright [Thomas Corneille](/wiki/Thomas_Corneille "Thomas Corneille") for his 1651 comedy *Feint Astrologue*.[[210]](#cite_note-212)

The most famous piece of music influenced by astrology is the orchestral suite *[The Planets](/wiki/The_Planets "The Planets")*. Written by the British composer [Gustav Holst](/wiki/Gustav_Holst "Gustav Holst") (1874–1934), and first performed in 1918, the framework of *The Planets* is based upon the astrological symbolism of the planets.[[211]](#cite_note-213) Each of the seven movements of the suite is based upon a different planet, though the movements are not in the order of the planets from the Sun. The composer [Colin Matthews](/wiki/Colin_Matthews "Colin Matthews") wrote an eighth movement entitled *Pluto, the Renewer*, first performed in 2000, as the suite was written prior to Pluto's discovery.[[212]](#cite_note-214) In 1937, another British composer, [Constant Lambert](/wiki/Constant_Lambert "Constant Lambert"), wrote a ballet on astrological themes, called *[Horoscope](/wiki/Horoscope_(ballet) "Horoscope (ballet)")*.[[213]](#cite_note-215) In 1974, the New Zealand composer [Edwin Carr](/wiki/Edwin_Carr_(composer) "Edwin Carr (composer)") wrote *The Twelve Signs: An Astrological Entertainment*[[214]](#cite_note-216) for orchestra without strings.[[215]](#cite_note-217) [Camille Paglia](/wiki/Camille_Paglia "Camille Paglia") acknowledges astrology as an influence on her work of literary criticism *[Sexual Personae](/wiki/Sexual_Personae "Sexual Personae")* (1990).[[216]](#cite_note-218) The American comedian [Harvey Sid Fisher](/wiki/Harvey_Sid_Fisher "Harvey Sid Fisher") is known for his comedic songs about astrology.[[217]](#cite_note-219)

Astrology features strongly in [Eleanor Catton](/wiki/Eleanor_Catton "Eleanor Catton")'s *[The Luminaries](/wiki/The_Luminaries "The Luminaries")*, recipient of the [2013 Man Booker Prize](/wiki/2013_Man_Booker_Prize "2013 Man Booker Prize").[[218]](#cite_note-220)

"The literary interest in astrology, which had been on the increase in England throughout the fourteenth century, culminated in the works of Gower and Chaucer. Although references to astrology were already frequent in the romances of the fourteenth century, these still retained the signs of being foreign importations. It was only in the fifteenth century that astrological similes and embellishments became a matter of course in the literature of England.  
     Such innovations, one must confess, were due far more to Chaucer than to Gower. Gower, too, saw artistic possibilities in the new astrological learning, and promptly used these in his retelling of the Alexander legend—but he confined himself, for the most part, to a bald rehearsal of facts and theories. It is, accordingly, as a part of the long encyclopaedia of natural science that he inserted into his *Confessio Amantis*, and in certain didactic passages of the *Vox Clamantis* and the *Mirour de l'Omme*, that Astrology figures most largely in his works ... Gower's sources on the subject of astrology ... were Albumasar's *Introductorium in Astronomiam*, the Pseudo-Aristotelian *Secretum Secretorum*, Brunetto Latini's *Trésor*, and the *Speculum Astronomiae* ascribed to Albert the Great."
